channel.title: Combined Economist RSS Feed
channel.description: Combined feed of multiple Economist RSS sources with archive.is/o/nuunc links

item.title: America’s foes see opportunity in Asia’s oil shock
item.description: Russia and China are quietly stepping in

item.title: Why women, more than men, are abandoning rural Japan
item.description: Bigger cities are, in turn, skewing more female

item.title: How the Gulf’s war is becoming Asia’s crisis too
item.description: Prices, debt, and scarcity will strike a blow against the world’s workshop

item.title: Scientists are working on “everything vaccines”
item.description: A single jab could protect against a wide range of pathogens

item.title: A trio of firms want to clean up steelmaking
item.description: The industry is currently responsible for around 8% of the world’s carbon emissions

item.title: Mark Carney’s party is poised to take control of Parliament
item.description: Canada’s fate will probably soon be squarely in Liberal hands

item.title: How China hopes to win from the war
item.description: Never interrupt your enemy when he is making a mistake

item.title: The Iran war hurts China less than its rivals but more than it admits
item.description: One upside could be a reluctant change in its growth model

item.title: How worried should you be about private credit?
item.description: Its humbling could raise borrowing costs

item.title: Private-credit funds are showing signs of strain
item.description: Some panicky investors are running for the exit

item.title: “Liberation Day” has reshaped trade—but not as Donald Trump hoped
item.description: In many ways, global commerce has strengthened

item.title: Can a country get too rich?
item.description: Norway shows the potential pitfalls of uncommon prosperity

item.title: “Liberation Year” has not freed American factories
item.description: Even the winners from Donald Trump’s trade war are feeling squeezed

item.title: The perils of a ground war in Iran
item.description: Donald Trump may send in troops. Does he know what to do with them?

item.title: Hurricane Trump threatens to blow China off course
item.description: Chinese elites do not agree on who may win from the war in Iran

item.title: Index providers should not bend the rules for Elon Musk
item.description: They will only expose ordinary investors to unnecessary risks

item.title: Coal is back in fashion
item.description: An LNG crunch is good news for the world’s dirtiest fuel

item.title: Is China covering up a violent attack at a Beijing market?
item.description: News outlets put a premium on making the country seem harmonious

item.title: Our prediction for how SCOTUS will rule on birthright citizenship
item.description: Will the Trump administration lose another high-profile case?

item.title: Binyamin Netanyahu is down—but not out
item.description: Ahead of an election due in October, a divided opposition and the war could hand him a lifeline

item.title: The War Room newsletter: Will Trump send troops into Iran?
item.description: The president must be aware that the political costs of a land war could be “extremely high”, writes Shashank Joshi, our defence editor

item.title: For China’s officials, the goal was once growth. Now it’s loyalty
item.description: An obsession with gdp is replaced by an obsession with Xi Jinping

item.title: Why a startup is teaching human brain cells to play “Doom”
item.description: They could herald a new type of computing

item.title: After Iran, gold is looking less glittery
item.description: Is the yellow metal the new crypto?

item.title: Donald Trump and the art of bad diplomacy
item.description: One of the advisers the president ignores is his younger self

item.title: The Venezuela Donald Trump “runs” is a land of surreal contrasts
item.description: Animal spirits are stirring. The opposition has hope. The regime’s softening of repression is uneven

item.title: All sides in the Gulf war are at risk of overplaying their hands
item.description: The costs of dragging out the conflict could be very high indeed

item.title: Is Bollywood’s latest megahit propaganda for Narendra Modi?
item.description: “Dhurandhar: The Revenge” portrays India extracting a price for Pakistani perfidy

item.title: The plan to make IPOs great again
item.description: America’s regulators and market operators are teaming up to rekindle public listings

item.title: China’s leadership is about to be shaken up
item.description: The changes will affect every level. Except one

item.title: How Iran is making a mint from Donald Trump’s war
item.description: China is helping the Revolutionary Guards profit from Iranian crude

item.title: The Houthis’ attack on Israel may point to escalation in the Iran war
item.description: Yemen’s Shia militia could threaten to choke the Red Sea

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Three problems with Trump’s Iran strategy
item.description: John Prideaux, our US editor, on the risks of the president’s approach to foreign policy

item.title: The nightmare scenario for global trade
item.description: There could be worse blockages than the Strait of Hormuz, our analysis finds

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: Advantage Iran
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed our cover

item.title: Should you track your VO2 max?
item.description: Treat it is a measure, not a target

item.title: The War Room newsletter: The war that shaped modern Iran
item.description: Iran’s conflict with Iraq in the 1980s provides valuable lessons for today, writes Richard Cockett, a senior editor

item.title: Hormuz is not the only weak spot for global trade
item.description: Many shipping routes are vulnerable, from the Strait of Malacca to the Panama Canal

item.title: England has shown the world how to replace farm subsidies
item.description: A rare Brexit dividend

item.title: How Chinese companies are reshaping Indonesia
item.description: They are fleeing cut-throat competition at home

item.title: Millions of Burmese struggle to find safety in Thailand
item.description: Many refugees are left exposed

item.title: China is breaking into one of the world’s weirdest car markets
item.description: An island of American motoring in Central Asia is changing fast

item.title: China’s huge pork industry is a victim of its own success
item.description: The country is awash in its favourite meat

item.title: Does the Iran war increase the risk of a Chinese attack on Taiwan?
item.description: China has reason to wait, but its window of opportunity has widened

item.title: Jiang Shengnan is the most vocal woman in Chinese politics
item.description: She is trying to push change from within the system

item.title: For Donald Trump, Cuba is everything Iran is not
item.description: A breakthrough is in his grasp. But his idea of progress may be a throwback to another era

item.title: Snarled airports and frozen funding test the new DHS secretary
item.description: Markwayne Mullin promises a kinder, gentler department

item.title: America’s spies have a lot to complain about
item.description: They are grappling with politicisation, purges and a president who may not want to hear them

item.title: The weekly cartoon
Business
Russia wants to limit contact with the outside world
item.description: A recent internet blackout reflects the Kremlin’s nervousness

item.title: Germany’s Social Democrats gaze into the abyss
item.description: But an electoral drubbing could open a window for national reforms

item.title: France offers some hope for defeating populists
item.description: The mainstream parties get the biggest prizes

item.title: The war’s biggest corporate winners and losers may surprise you
item.description: Markets are beginning to signal the long-term consequences of the surge in fuel prices

item.title: Will the EU’s new merger rules unleash a wave of dealmaking?
item.description: Don’t hold your breath

item.title: A new case of chip smuggling shows the limits of export controls
item.description: Contraband AI gear continues to make its way to China

item.title: Welcome to emoji school
item.description: OK boomers! Your questions answered

item.title: Iran’s regime walls off the internet
item.description: The latest shutdown looks designed to be permanent

item.title: Israeli settlers are growing more violent in the West Bank
item.description: Since the war with Iran began, attacks against Palestinians have become more systematic

item.title: In the current Gulf war, water may prove as decisive as oil
item.description: The Arab states are plainly vulnerable, but so is Iran

item.title: Brazil has a secret weapon against oil shocks
item.description: Biofuels will help the country fend off the effects of conflict in the Middle East

item.title: Mexico’s broken economy
item.description: Claudia Sheinbaum’s government is tinkering, not reforming

item.title: Politics
China is winning the AI talent race
item.description: Its lead over the West is only set to widen

item.title: How long will Israel stay in Lebanon?
item.description: The threat of another occupation is tearing at the fabric of Lebanese society

item.title: The end of the world’s longest-running Maoist insurgency
item.description: A report from one of the Naxalites’ final redoubts

item.title: Donald Trump says he is close to a deal with Iran
item.description: Almost no one believes that talks are getting anywhere

item.title: Advantage Iran
item.description: A month of bombing has achieved nothing. Will Donald Trump escalate, or talk?

item.title: The decline and fall of the Roman currency empire
item.description: The fate of ancient coins offers uncomfortable lessons about dollar dominance

item.title: Europe should think twice before weakening its merger rules
item.description: A strict competition policy is not the barrier to bigger firms

item.title: The case against energy bail-outs
item.description: As war rages in Iran, governments must not repeat the mistakes of 2022

item.title: Christine Lagarde’s sober tone on the Gulf war energy shock
item.description: Our interview with the ECB’s president

item.title: The human toll of the Iran war, in charts and maps
item.description: Our analysis suggests that more than 22m people across the Middle East live near a reported strike

item.title: America’s pro-Israel lobby is facing a backlash
item.description: As attitudes to Israel shift, AIPAC is shifting its tactics

item.title: Meta and Google face a reckoning over social-media addiction
item.description: A landmark verdict in California could have far-reaching consequences

item.title: NASA’s Moon-base plans mark a rethinking of its future
item.description: The changes are welcome

item.title: How much will America’s oilmen benefit from the Iran war?
item.description: Less than the Trump administration thinks

item.title: Big food’s troubles go from bad to worse
item.description: High prices and healthier diets are gnawing away at sales

item.title: Amazon’s unprecedented gamble on AI redemption might just work
item.description: Call it “The Bet-Everything Store”

item.title: Europe’s populist right should be outvoted rather than ostracised
item.description: Imagining there’s no fascists is a bad strategy

item.title: The Revolutionary Guards are taking over Iran
item.description: They now appear to run both the state and the war

item.title: Mexico must unleash its private sector
item.description: Claudia Sheinbaum’s biggest problem is weak investment and growth, not Donald Trump

item.title: New research uncovers more of the story of man’s best friend
item.description: The evolutionary tale of Europe’s dogs is becoming clearer

item.title: Early French winemakers had surprisingly sophisticated techniques
item.description: Genomics suggests the country has been making wine for over 2,500 years

item.title: The world’s most unaffordable housing is not where you think
item.description: Asia’s great cities will founder without more reasonably priced homes

item.title: Autonomous swarms are the future of drone warfare
item.description: Much of the innovation is being carried out in Ukraine

item.title: Markets are gripped by an alarming cognitive dissonance
item.description: Investors all seem to think everyone else is wrong

item.title: China’s government both drives and constrains the rise of AI
item.description: Officials want to lead the world in the new technology, but are still wary of disruption

item.title: What a battle to reopen the Strait of Hormuz would look like
item.description: A lot of ships, aircraft and soldiers would have to spend a long time in harm’s way for uncertain results

item.title: Botswana prepares to take an even bigger gamble on diamonds
item.description: The African country wants to own more of De Beers, the firm that helped it to escape poverty

item.title: China’s new masterplan for its tech economy in 2030 and beyond
item.description: The Communist Party’s technological ambition is breathtaking

item.title: Giorgia Meloni’s big setback in Italy
item.description: Her failed referendum gamble hints at growing dissatisfaction with her government

item.title: How high could global inflation go?
item.description: With luck, the Iran war won’t cause a recession. But the surge in energy prices will push up the cost of living

item.title: ByteDance is swallowing the internet—in China and beyond
item.description: Can anything stop its stunning rise?

item.title: Donald Trump’s latest climbdown suggests he wants to end the war
item.description: The divide between Israel and America is widening

item.title: The War Room newsletter: Trump is scrambling for options in Iran
item.description: “To end the war on current terms would look like defeat [for Trump] to most of the world,” writes Shashank Joshi, our defence editor

item.title: Marco Rubio, the chameleon in the war room
item.description: On Venezuela, Iran and Cuba, Donald Trump is listening to his secretary of state

item.title: Why the number of Islamic schools in Canada is soaring
item.description: Some Muslim parents worry about their children losing touch with their faith

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Donald Trump’s risky obsession with oil
item.description: Charlotte Howard, our New York bureau chief, argues the president’s energy agenda may yet damage his legacy

item.title: Ukraine’s top drone commander wants to bleed Russia’s army dry
item.description: “Madyar” Brovdi has pioneered unmanned warfare methods NATO is yet to learn

item.title: Donald Trump has four bad options for the war in Iran
item.description: None is guaranteed to end the war or ease the economic crisis it has produced

item.title: Why Bangalore has India’s best billionaires
item.description: Its philanthropists want to do more than just give money away

item.title: Why Donald Trump is putting his face on a coin
item.description: Money and power belong together

item.title: America goes on cyber-offence
item.description: Its aggressive new strategy comes as Iranian hackers threaten cyber-warfare

item.title: Westerners are fleeing their countries in record numbers
item.description: This will have economic consequences for the places they flee and their destinations

item.title: Even the best-case scenario for energy markets is disastrous
item.description: Whatever happens, high prices will outlive the Iran war

item.title: Is playing music good for the brain?
item.description: It would seem so, even for amateurs

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: Operation Blind Fury
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed this week’s cover

item.title: Tucker Carlson on whether Donald Trump has betrayed his base
item.description: And why MAGA must look toward Europe

item.title: Africa after aid is more resilient than you might think
item.description: But more needs to be done to ensure a prosperous future

item.title: Gas will not be killed off by renewables any time soon
item.description: But there are ways to rely less on it

item.title: Lebanon’s leaders must take on Hizbullah
item.description: And Israel must not play the spoiler

item.title: Life in Myanmar’s biggest city is increasingly grim
item.description: A new rubber-stamp parliament will solve nothing

item.title: Why China’s fight on air pollution has slowed
item.description: Officials are now trying to take it to the next stage

item.title: Public opinion in China is hardening on America and Taiwan
item.description: A rare poll shows the views of ordinary Chinese are changing

item.title: The future of Africa will be shaped by investment rather than aid
item.description: Foreign and African firms are giving the continent reasons for hope

item.title: American farmers are feeling the squeeze
item.description: Surging fertiliser prices are a blow to a limping industry

item.title: Is an obsession with immigration leaving America exposed?
item.description: The Iran war raises questions about the country’s counterterrorism capabilities

item.title: The weekly cartoon
Business
Politics
Viktor Orban’s pro-natalist policies are not working
item.description: Despite lavish spending on mothers, Hungary’s fertility rate is falling again

item.title: The Iran war is forcing Europe to confront its energy problem
item.description: High power prices are putting the continent’s economy at risk

item.title: The secrets to a good employee survey
item.description: Likert or not

item.title: The Iran war casts a shadow over BASF’s nascent revival
item.description: The turnaround of the German chemicals giant is still a work in progress

item.title: Israel contemplates a ground invasion of Lebanon
item.description: That could wreck Lebanon’s chances of taking on Hizbullah itself

item.title: A shake-up at Africa’s spikiest media group
item.description: Can the new owner preserve its independent spirit?

item.title: The war in eastern Congo is escalating far from view
item.description: Fighting in South Kivu is a disaster for civilians and may threaten the copper belt

item.title: Cuba’s broken economy leaves it at Donald Trump’s mercy
item.description: He seems more interested in creating a client state than a free Cuba

item.title: There is plenty of scope for the Iran war to intensify
item.description: But no obvious way to bring it to an end

item.title: A deadly strike in Kabul could have big knock-on effects
item.description: The war between Afghanistan and Pakistan is growing ever more lethal

item.title: Why AI has not yet upset India’s IT industry
item.description: Deploying the technology is the real world is proving tricky

item.title: Panicked Indians are scrambling to buy gas
item.description: War over Iran threatens the fuel that powers hundreds of millions of stoves

item.title: America may be a petrostate. But the energy shock still hurts
item.description: And further angers economically frustrated citizens

item.title: Which country is the biggest loser from the energy shock?
item.description: We rank the poor world’s exposure and buffers

item.title: The new economics of sex work
item.description: As the sex economy grows, it deserves serious analysis

item.title: War in Iran is making Donald Trump weaker—and angrier
item.description: By diminishing the president’s political superpowers, his reckless campaign may make him more dangerous

item.title: Does Donald Trump even care about the midterms?
item.description: He is more focused on dominating his party, and on his place in history

item.title: Elliott Management and the art of telling bosses they’re wrong
item.description: The hedge fund has industrialised shareholder activism

item.title: The Anglosphere is increasingly miserable
item.description: The World Happiness Report shows it diverging from the rest of the world

item.title: The Iran war could sap American military power for years
item.description: It is devouring munitions and exhausting an already stretched navy

item.title: What if Donald Trump decided to ban oil exports?
item.description: Trying to keep prices low that way could backfire spectacularly

item.title: How Ukraine and Europe got caught in a geopolitical lovers’ tiff
item.description: A testing moment for steadfast allies

item.title: How the Iran war is hurting Donald Trump
item.description: An unpopular conflict and costly fuel could hobble his presidency

item.title: How Zara fought off H&M and Shein
item.description: A touch of luxury has helped

item.title: Is cheap energy the key to China gaining AI supremacy?
item.description: Leaders are hoping it will make up for its weakness in chips

item.title: The next phase of artificial intelligence may require very different processors
item.description: The GPUs that powered the AI boom can’t handle the workload

item.title: China is a serious contender in the race for fusion energy
item.description: Could it be the first to build a commercial reactor?

item.title: Top AI models underperform in languages other than English
item.description: Those turning to them for health advice are most at risk

item.title: A dirty deal with Cuba would be better than the alternatives
item.description: A prolonged blockade risks creating a humanitarian crisis on America’s doorstep

item.title: Middle East Dispatch newsletter: Iran’s mood shifts
item.description: The immediate imperative is no longer political change but personal safety, writes Nick Pelham, our Middle East correspondent

item.title: A spy scandal upends Slovenia’s election campaign
item.description: Embarrassing videos and a mysterious Israeli intelligence firm

item.title: Nvidia is expanding its empire
item.description: The AI boom’s biggest winner moves beyond chips

item.title: The assassination of senior leaders weakens Iran—but at a cost
item.description: The regime is becoming ever less predictable

item.title: Will South Korea’s epic bull market survive the energy shock?
item.description: Probably, but investors should brace for more volatility

item.title: China cannot escape the energy shock
item.description: Despite renewables and reserves, it will suffer

item.title: America’s failing gunboat diplomacy
item.description: Like some fusty old imperialist, Donald Trump is flummoxed by foreigners

item.title: Africa’s richest man has ambitious plans for the continent
item.description: But the tycoon also has his critics

item.title: Are there enough missile interceptors?
item.description: Shortages in Israel and the Gulf could determine the war’s course

item.title: The Iran war is roiling commodities far beyond oil
item.description: Shortages of fuels and chemicals threaten industries from farming to pharmaceuticals

item.title: War may bring lasting change to the airline business
item.description: Western carriers will relish the chance to win some of their customers back

item.title: The quiet recovery of Ireland’s ancient tongue
item.description: In the land of St Patrick, Irish is making a steady comeback

item.title: Rapid-charging EV batteries are on the way
item.description: They can be topped up in as little time as a tank of fuel

item.title: Will America’s Asian allies get dragged into the Iran war?
item.description: They are worried Uncle Sam will abandon them if they don’t fight

item.title: The War Room newsletter: A conflict Trump was ill-prepared for
item.description: There is no simple way to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, writes Shashank Joshi, our defence editor

item.title: A Maoist survival guide to the Iranian energy crisis
item.description: For a self-reliant power like China, no supplier is ever irreplaceable

item.title: Why the Iran crisis caught Europe flat-footed
item.description: The EU suffers the war’s fallout, but can do little to cut it short

item.title: The Iran war may be about to escalate
item.description: Gulf states could join the conflict

item.title: A muddled war and rising prices are boosting Democrats’ midterm hopes
item.description: As the conflict continues, the party is sharpening its response

item.title: Trouble is brewing among America’s corporate borrowers
item.description: Surging energy prices will add to fears of a wave of defaults

item.title: Open-source intelligence shuts down
item.description: Satellite images of the Middle East are suddenly disappearing

item.title: What Nitish Kumar did for Bihar, India’s poorest state
item.description: The state’s longest-serving chief minister raised voters’ expectations of their politicians

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: An attack on the world economy
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed this week’s cover

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Why America isn’t talking about the Iran war
item.description: John Prideaux, our US editor, on a nation that is waiting and seeing

item.title: Vladimir Putin enjoys a huge windfall from the Iran war
item.description: But the sugar high may not last

item.title: Gulf states are burning through interceptors
item.description: The pace far outstrips that seen in Ukraine. Stocks are under severe pressure

item.title: Should you take GLP-1 drugs for longevity?
item.description: The evidence is tantalising. But that is not the same as proof

item.title: How wars are adding hours to your flights
item.description: Our analysis shows which routes are getting longer

item.title: China is wrestling with a novel phenomenon: inherited wealth
item.description: Even as the economy slows and opportunity narrows, a lucky few receive big windfalls

item.title: Haiti needs order first, then elections
item.description: Voters must be able to turn out without risking death

item.title: How to teach Donald Trump a Latin lesson
item.description: By alienating Hispanics, he has given Democrats an open goal

item.title: China’s hereditary elite is taking shape
item.description: The Communist Party is afraid to tax inherited wealth

item.title: Islamists woo Bangladesh with everything but Islam
item.description: They are a hit on campus, too

item.title: Taiwan’s bid to export drones free of Chinese parts is taking off
item.description: The island’s unmanned aerial vehicles are already in use on Ukraine’s front line

item.title: Nepal’s new prime minister is a 35-year-old former rapper
item.description: Voters have given Balendra Shah an unprecedented mandate and enormous power

item.title: Seoul’s housing market is a huge political and economic headache
item.description: Lee Jae Myung is staking his presidency on fixing it

item.title: China’s nationalist spy thriller has few girls and lots of government
item.description: “Main melody” films are in a rut

item.title: Hong Kong’s property market has turned
item.description: That’s not good news for everyone

item.title: Some of China’s officials are becoming social-media stars
item.description: It may help them get promotions

item.title: America’s blame-Israel lobby
item.description: If the Iran war goes wrong, MAGA will want a culprit who is not named Donald Trump

item.title: Two very different states take aim at soaring hospital prices
item.description: But are price controls the answer?

item.title: Why investors won’t know what to make of AI for a while
item.description: Markets always struggle to price technological revolutions

item.title: Business
The weekly cartoon
Politics
A popular German Green wins a surprise victory
item.description: Cem Özdemir becomes the first Turkish-German to run a state

item.title: In Paris’s mayoral race, it’s drivers against cyclists
item.description: A decade of greening leaves the capital less congested but more divided

item.title: As war rages, Turkey’s strongman puts the opposition on trial
item.description: Chaos in the Middle East makes President Erdogan indispensable to the West

item.title: In praise of grunt work
item.description: AI promises to eliminate drudgery. Careful what you wish for

item.title: Altman, Amodei and Musk fight dirty for the biggest prize in business
item.description: Never before has so much money been sought from investors in a single year

item.title: Kenya’s ailing sugar sector is a test case for reform
item.description: Low productivity and political corruption bedevil the whole economy

item.title: What data reveal about the war’s progress
item.description: We analyse 1,615 distinct attacks, and more besides

item.title: Iran’s praetorian guard may leave the war diminished but undefeated
item.description: Even if it will struggle to rebuild its capabilities

item.title: Who is Iran’s new leader?
item.description: No one knows if he will last

item.title: Brazilian cinema is having its moment
item.description: Thank growing competition and a more internationally minded Academy awards

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: How baseball created 20th-century America
item.description: Jon Fasman, our senior culture correspondent, explores the making of a national pastime in an age of immigration and industrialisation

item.title: America’s war on Iran may bring Bahrain to its knees
item.description: The tiny, indebted kingdom is seeing its main sources of revenue dry up

item.title: The damage to the world economy from the Iran war will be severe, but uneven
item.description: It has already caused the biggest energy supply shock in history

item.title: In Trump’s world, companies seek insurance against political risk
item.description: It could soon get more expensive

item.title: An attack on the world economy
item.description: Whatever happens in the Strait of Hormuz, energy markets have been changed for ever

item.title: There are no good options for Iran’s nuclear programme
item.description: If America cannot eliminate the threat, what should it do?

item.title: How America and Israel built vast military targeting machines
item.description: Software is supercharging the process of finding things to bomb

item.title: Why corporate lawyers always win
item.description: The world is a pedant’s paradise

item.title: Viktor Orban’s illiberal intellectual patronage system
item.description: The Budapest Megaphone blares populism to the world

item.title: Want to hack your body with peptides? If only the science agreed
item.description: Boosters say they will do everything from aiding strength, recovery and longevity

item.title: How Gap is trying to get its cool back
item.description: Its boss is using the Barbie formula to turn around the ailing fashion label

item.title: AI is helping expand the frontier of theoretical physics
item.description: It is blurring the line between tool and collaborator

item.title: Could special forces truly obliterate Iran’s nuclear programme?
item.description: Swiping the country’s uranium would require one of the largest raids in military history

item.title: At last, Haiti has some hope
item.description: Aggressive policing is weakening gangsters’ control

item.title: Analysing Africa newsletter: The real meaning of the Iran war for Africa
item.description: John McDermott, our chief Africa correspondent, writes about the Middle East crisis

item.title: Liquefied natural gas: the overlooked economic chokepoint
item.description: Alternatives to Gulf supplies are scarce

item.title: Can America clear the Strait of Hormuz of Iran’s drones and mines?
item.description: Escorting convoys of oil tanker with warships may give Iran juicy American targets

item.title: India has much to lose from a world in chaos
item.description: Not long ago, India was cocky about handling an unpredictable America. No longer

item.title: Donald Trump’s options to cool oil prices are sorely limited
item.description: He says he has a plan. It would need to be very cunning

item.title: Time to buy the most rubbish stocks you can find
item.description: The dash for trash

item.title: A new wave of disrupters takes on American health care
item.description: Patients are unhappy. Can AI help?

item.title: “Trophy wives” are out of fashion
item.description: Our analysis finds that age gaps among America’s richest couples are shrinking

item.title: Ukraine’s housing market is increasingly peculiar
item.description: With blood in the streets, many are buying property

item.title: Lost Latino love could cost Republicans the midterms
item.description: Trump’s much-vaunted success with Hispanics is turning to ashes

item.title: America’s war aims may be diverging from Israel’s
item.description: For Binyamin Netanyahu they are, as ever, a question of politics

item.title: The War Room newsletter: How the Iran war is escalating
item.description: Shashank Joshi, our defence editor, explains that the conflict is intensifying in four ways

item.title: The view from Tehrangeles
item.description: The Iranian diaspora cheered Khamenei’s death. They are divided over what should come next

item.title: The Iran energy shock reverberates across financial markets
item.description: For investors, the war is like the invasion of Ukraine all over again

item.title: Should the Gulf states join attacks on Iran?
item.description: A lack of trust in America and Israel, internal divisions and the risk of domestic strife all hamper a unified response

item.title: China’s AI giants are handing out cash to lure in users
item.description: How long can their ferocious rivalry last?

item.title: There are 56 ethnicities in China—and 55 are getting squashed
item.description: China wants its minorities decorative, but not distinct

item.title: Iran’s defiant regime picks a new supreme leader
item.description: The appointment suggests the Revolutionary Guards have the upper hand
